title: Managing Asbestos in Water Damage Jobs — Safe Work Australia Obligations for Restoration Technicians
description: Understand your legal obligations when asbestos is encountered on a water damage restoration job in Australia — Safe Work Australia regulations, licensed removalists, notification requirements, and documentation. Critical knowledge for any Australian restorer.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Asbestos and Water Damage in Australia

Australia has one of the highest per-capita rates of asbestos-related disease in the world, largely due to the widespread use of asbestos-containing materials (ACMs) in residential and commercial construction through the 1970s and 1980s. Any building constructed or renovated before 1990 is considered high-risk for ACM presence; buildings constructed before 2004 may also contain ACMs if older materials were reused.

Water damage restoration technicians working in these buildings must understand their legal obligations, because disturbing ACMs — even inadvertently — has serious legal and health consequences.

## Identifying Suspected ACMs

Common ACMs in Australian residential buildings include:

- **Fibro sheeting** (flat or corrugated asbestos cement sheeting) — eaves, external cladding, internal walls in older homes
- **Textured ceiling coatings** — Artex-style coatings applied before 1990
- **Vinyl floor tiles and adhesive** — particularly 225 mm and 300 mm square vinyl tiles
- **Backing compounds** and some pipe lagging
- **Roof shingles** in older residential properties

If you encounter material that may contain asbestos, do not disturb it. You are not required to confirm through testing before stopping work — the obligation is to treat suspected ACMs as if they contain asbestos until confirmed otherwise by a qualified assessor.

## Legal Obligations Under Australian WHS Laws

Under the model Work Health and Safety Regulations (adopted in all Australian states and territories), removal of friable asbestos (asbestos that can be crumbled by hand) requires a **Class A** licensed asbestos removalist. Removal of non-friable asbestos (bonded, in good condition) in amounts greater than 10 m² requires a **Class B** licensed removalist.

Restoration technicians who are not licensed removalists must stop work and engage a licensed removalist before proceeding with any scope item that involves removing or disturbing suspected ACMs. This applies even if the ACM was not originally part of the restoration scope — for example, if drying equipment disturbs fibro eaves sheeting.

**Notification requirements**: Removal of more than 10 m² of non-friable ACM, or any amount of friable ACM, requires prior notification to the relevant state/territory work health and safety regulator.

## Impact on the Insurance Claim

The presence of asbestos significantly affects scope and cost. The insurer must be notified as soon as ACMs are identified, and the scope must be revised to include:

- Asbestos assessment fee (qualified hygienist)
- Licensed removalist labour and disposal (per m² or per item)
- Air monitoring during and after removal
- Clearance inspection by a licensed hygienist
- Additional containment costs

Asbestos removal and clearance documentation must be attached to the insurance claim. Policies differ on coverage — some explicitly cover ACM removal; others require a separate ACM endorsement. Advise the insured to check their policy before commencing remediation.

## Practical Steps for Restorers

1. Complete an asbestos visual assessment before commencing any invasive work in pre-1990 buildings
2. Note suspected ACMs in the inspection report with photos and location references
3. Do not drill, cut, sand, or break suspected ACMs
4. Notify the insurer and engage a hygienist for confirmation testing
5. Obtain a clearance certificate from the licensed hygienist after removal before resuming restoration work
6. Retain all asbestos documentation with the claim file
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're covering something that every restoration technician in Australia absolutely must understand: your obligations when you encounter asbestos on a water damage job.

This is not a topic where you can afford to wing it. Disturbing asbestos-containing materials — ACMs — even accidentally, can result in serious health consequences for you and your crew, and significant legal liability under Australian work health and safety laws.

First, the risk profile. Any building built or renovated before 1990 is high risk for ACMs. Buildings before 2004 may have them if older materials were reused. In residential water damage work, you'll most commonly encounter fibro sheeting — that flat asbestos cement on eaves, sheds, and internal walls in older homes. Also watch for textured ceiling coatings applied before 1990, vinyl floor tiles in 225 or 300 millimetre squares, and vinyl adhesive backing.

If you encounter material you suspect contains asbestos, stop work on that element. You don't need a lab result to stop — treat it as ACM until a qualified assessor confirms otherwise. That's the legal standard.

Here's the critical legal line in Australia. Removal of friable asbestos — the stuff that crumbles in your hand — requires a Class A licensed removalist. Removal of non-friable ACM — bonded, in good condition — requires a Class B licence if the area is more than 10 square metres. If you're not licenced, you cannot do the removal, full stop.

And if you're removing more than 10 square metres of non-friable ACM, or any amount of friable ACM, you need to notify your state or territory work health and safety regulator before you start. The notification requirement often surprises people.

On the insurance side: notify the insurer as soon as you identify suspected ACMs. The scope needs to be revised to include the hygienist assessment, licensed removal, air monitoring, and a clearance certificate. Not all policies cover ACM removal — advise your client to check their policy.

Before any invasive work in a pre-1990 building, complete an asbestos visual assessment, photograph any suspected materials, and note them in your inspection report. In RestoreAssist, the initial inspection checklist includes an ACM flag so this step is built into the workflow.

Head to restoreassist.app for more.
